title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Germany — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Order coffee by type, not size
items.text: Germans order by what they want—Espresso, Cappuccino, Americano—rather than small/medium/large. A standard Kaffee is a small black coffee; ask for Verlängerter if you want it slightly longer. Sizes vary by café, so don't assume standardization.

items.label: Café culture means sitting, not rushing
items.text: Germans linger in cafés for hours with one coffee. Waiters won't rush you or bring the bill unless asked. This is a place to read, work, or chat—not grab-and-go like some countries.

items.label: Avoid chain cafés in city centers
items.text: Starbucks exists but locals prefer independent Cafés or traditional Konditorei (bakery-cafés). Look for Wiener Melange in Vienna-style cafés for a proper cappuccino equivalent.

items.label: Kaffee mit Kuchen (coffee with cake)
items.text: The classic 3 p.m. ritual—a small coffee paired with a slice of Schwarzwälder Kirschtorte, Käsekuchen (cheesecake), or Apfelstrudel. Most cafés have excellent house-made cakes.

items.label: Schnitzel (breaded cutlet)
items.text: Thin-pounded pork or veal, breaded and fried golden. Serve with Zitrone (lemon) or Preiselbeersauce (lingonberry sauce). Order Wiener Schnitzel for veal, Schweineschnitzel for pork.

items.label: Spätzle (egg noodles)
items.text: Soft, irregular handmade noodles from Swabia, often topped with Käse (cheese) to become Käsespätzle. A comfort-food staple, especially in the south and Baden-Württemberg.

items.label: Sauerbraten (pot roast)
items.text: Beef marinated in vinegar and spices for days, then slow-cooked until tender. Traditionally served with red cabbage and potato dumplings—a winter must-try.

items.label: Currywurst (curry sausage)
items.text: Sliced grilled Bratwurst smothered in tangy curry ketchup, typically eaten standing at a street stand with fries. Berlin claims ownership; grab it at any Imbiss (fast food stand).

items.label: Schweinshaxe (pork knuckle)
items.text: A massive roasted pork leg, crispy-skinned and fall-apart tender, served with sauerkraut and mustard. Most famous in Bavaria; order it only if truly hungry.

items.label: Flammkuchen (Alsatian tart)
items.text: Crispy thin crust topped with crème fraîche, onion, and bacon—not quite pizza, not quite bread. A specialty along the France-Germany border, best eaten fresh from a wood oven.

items.label: Tagesgerichte are the best value
items.text: Most restaurants display daily specials (often a main with soup and bread) at significantly lower prices than the regular menu. These are usually very fresh and what locals eat.

items.label: Watch for portion sizes on the menu
items.text: German restaurants often list portion size (e.g., 200g) next to dishes. Main courses can be enormous; don't be shy about ordering just a starter and salad if you're not very hungry.

items.label: Beilagen (sides) cost extra
items.text: A main dish often comes with just the protein; vegetables, potatoes, or bread are listed separately and charged as Beilage. Check the menu carefully to avoid surprise costs.

items.label: Vegetarian options exist but aren't extensive
items.text: Germany is improving, but traditional restaurants still have limited meat-free choices. Look for Käsespätzle, Pilzrisotto, or Kartoffelsuppe; bigger cities have dedicated vegetarian restaurants.

items.label: Suppe (soup) is often a starter, not a main
items.text: German soups are typically light and come before the main course. Eintopf, however, is a hearty one-pot meal served as a main (often the cheapest option on the menu).

items.label: Gasthaus/Gasthof (traditional inn)
items.text: Family-run restaurants serving hearty, honest German food at reasonable prices. These are where locals eat; menus rarely change, quality is consistent, and atmosphere is warm and unpretentious.

items.label: Biergarten (beer garden)
items.text: Outdoor communal seating, self-service or table service, serving beer, sausages, and simple food. Most famous in Bavaria, but found in every city; bring your own food or eat theirs—either is acceptable.

items.label: Imbiss (street food stand)
items.text: Quick, cheap, standing-room-only counter serving Currywurst, Döner, Falafel, and grilled sausages. Perfect for lunch or late-night bites; quality varies wildly but often excellent.

items.label: Mensa (university canteen)
items.text: Open to non-students at lunch, offering subsidized meals (5–8€) of decent quality. Not fancy, but you'll eat alongside students at rock-bottom prices.

items.label: Weinstube (wine bar)
items.text: Cozy, often family-owned spots focused on wine (especially in wine regions like Mosel or Rheingau) with simple regional food. Expect excellent pairings and knowledgeable staff.

items.label: Avoid tourist-trap restaurants near major sights
items.text: Restaurants with picture menus and multilingual staff near train stations, cathedral squares, and monuments charge 2–3× normal prices for mediocre food. Walk 5–10 minutes away and quality and value improve dramatically.

items.label: Order beer by region and style
items.text: Pilsner dominates in the north, Weizen (wheat beer) in Bavaria, Dunkelweizen in darker forms. In some regions, ask for a Helles (pale lager) or ask the bartender what's local—never assume nationwide consistency.

items.label: Pint glasses are rare; beer comes in specific sizes
items.text: Standard is 0.5L (called Maß in Bavaria), but also 0.3L, 0.4L, or even 0.2L. Just say the size; don't ask for 'a beer' and expect to know what you'll get.

items.label: Weißbier (Bavarian wheat beer)
items.text: Cloudy, fruity, slightly sweet wheat beer, traditionally served in tall 0.5L glasses. Quintessential Bavarian summer drink; try brands like Schneider or Erdinger.

items.label: German wine is underrated and excellent
items.text: Rieslings from the Mosel, Rhine, and Rheingau regions are world-class and affordable. German labels look confusing (Kabinett, Spätlese, Auslese indicate sweetness/ripeness), but staff can explain; don't fear them.

items.label: Aperol Spritz is popular but not traditional
items.text: Germans enjoy local sparkling wines (Sekt), but Aperol Spritz has become trendy in cities. For authenticity, order Sekt, Glühwein (mulled wine in winter), or stick to beer.

items.label: Drinking culture is social, not about getting drunk
items.text: Germans nurse beers for hours and often don't drink heavily. Binge drinking exists but isn't the norm; expect measured consumption and conversation.

items.label: The waiter won't bring the bill until you ask
items.text: Saying 'Die Rechnung, bitte' ('The bill, please') is essential. Germans find it rude if staff rush you; taking your time is normal and expected, even in busy restaurants.

items.label: Tipping is expected but modest
items.text: Round up to the nearest euro or leave 5–10% for good service. Say 'Stimmt so' (keep the change) when paying if you're rounding up; Germans don't typically use tip jars.

items.label: Elbows on the table during eating are fine; keep hands visible
items.text: Unlike some cultures, resting elbows while eating is acceptable in Germany. However, hands should be visible on the table, never in your lap (that's considered suspicious).

items.label: Compliment the cook if eating well
items.text: If food is exceptional, saying 'Das schmeckt mir sehr gut' (it tastes very good to me) or 'Kompliment an den Koch' (compliments to the cook) is warmly received.

items.label: Splitting bills is common and straightforward
items.text: Germans don't shy away from going Dutch (Spaltung, 'splitting'). Just ask the waiter; they're used to it and will calculate per person without awkwardness.

items.label: Reservations are wise, especially outside tourist areas
items.text: Good local restaurants fill up, and walk-ins may wait 30+ minutes. Call ahead or use online booking (TheFork is popular); it costs nothing and guarantees a table.

items.label: Bavarian: Leberkäse and Obatzda
items.text: Leberkäse is a seasoned meatloaf served warm with mustard and bread; Obatzda is a pungent cheese spread with paprika and onions. Both are Munich classics, best at traditional beer halls.

items.label: Swabian: Maultaschen (German ravioli)
items.text: Stuffed pasta parcels filled with meat, spinach, and breadcrumbs, often served in broth or with lentil soup. A hearty, delicious specialty from Baden-Württemberg.

items.label: Rhineland: Sauerbraten and Himmel und Erde
items.text: Himmel und Erde (Heaven and Earth) pairs mashed potatoes with stewed apples and onions, often topped with fried blood sausage. A sweet-savory comfort food unique to the region.

items.label: Berlin: Eisbein and Pigeon Pea Soup
items.text: Eisbein is a boiled pork knuckle served with sauerkraut and mustard—simple, heavy, and very Berlin. Pair with Erbsensuppe (pea soup) for an authentic working-class meal.

items.label: Hamburg: Labskaus (sailor's stew)
items.text: A rough hash of corned beef, potatoes, and herring, topped with a fried egg and served with pickled herring on the side. Looks odd, tastes hearty; order it for the story.
